MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT /Adjudication
Off Calendar Nov-01-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Michael Brooks Carroll, Esq. 415-788-7600 for Plaintiff.
Narendra Sharma, 707-386-4316 Defendant in Pro Per.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court announces it did not receive an email contesting the matter. Defendant argues Promissory note. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)
The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT NARENDRA SHARMA's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT and or in alternative for summary Adjudication.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Defendant Sharma's motion for summary judgment or adjudication is denied. Defendant fails to maintain his summary judgment burden per Code of Civil Procedure, 437c(p)(2). The gist of the complaint is that plaintiff agreed to lend $15,000 to defendant pursuant to a promissory note and plaintiff ultimately lent $16,000 to defendant per the same terms. (Complaint, 7-8.) Defendant's paltry separate statement of undisputed material facts fails to show that plaintiff's claim "cannot be established" within the meaning of Code of Civil Procedure, 437c(p)(2). Defendant "does not deny receiving two separate loans from plaintiff, for $13,000 and $3,000" (Reply at 1), and the signature on the promissory note appears identical to the signature on his declaration. There exist issues of fact regarding whether and when Defendant executed the promissory note, the terms and conditions of the parties' agreement, and whether the promissory note was supposed to apply to the $16,000 loan.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Aside Void Default Judgment Exceeding The Amount Demanded In Complaint
Off Calendar Aug-07-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Narendra Sharma, Tel. No. 1-707-386-4316, Defendant appearing in pro per;
No other appearances on the matter.

RULING - The court called Plaintiff's counsel twice by way of telephone call and since the court did not hear from Ms. Patel whether she is contesting or accepting the Amended Judgment, the court adopted its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Narenda Sharma's motion to set aside the judgment filed February 8, 2018 is granted unless plaintiff Vimlaben Patel agrees to entry of an amended judgment superseding the February 8 judgment in the amount of $21,325. Even if the February 8 judgment is not void, allowing it to remain in effect violates Mr. Patel's due process rights and would be a miscarriage of justice. The principal and prejudgment interest amounts in the February 8 judgment cannot be reconciled with Ms. Patel's verified complaint and motion for judgment on the pleadings. Moreover, the large increase in attorney's fees from the filing of the motion for judgment on the pleadings to the uncontested trial is not supported by anything in the record. Per the verified complaint and motion for judgment on the pleadings, Ms. Patel is at most entitled to a judgment of $21,325, comprised of principal of $12,500, prejudgment interest of $6,250, and attorney's fees of $2,575. Arguably no fees should be included in the amended judgment since the CCP and CRC provide that the setting of contractual fees should be done on a post-judgment motion, not at trial. The court has assumed that an additional $1000 in fees was incurred from the filing of the motion for judgment on the pleadings to the uncontested trial. If Ms. Patel agrees to the amended judgment by submitting a proposed amended judgment in conformity with this tentative ruling at or before the hearing, this motion will be denied. Otherwise, the February 8 judgment will be set aside and a new trial date will be set. Court to prepare order. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR# 8600 =302/hk
